summary:
The Mexican-American War, fought from April 1846 to February 1848, was an armed conflict between the United States and Mexico that resulted in a decisive American victory and the acquisition of more than half of Mexico's territory — including California, New Mexico, Arizona, Nevada, Utah, and Colorado. It was one of the most consequential and controversial wars in American history, condemned by critics including Ulysses S. Grant and Abraham Lincoln as an unjust war of conquest.

The immediate cause was a border dispute following the American annexation of Texas in 1845. Mexico had never recognized Texas independence, and when the United States claimed the Rio Grande as the Texas-Mexico boundary (Mexico insisted on the Nueces River further north), President James K. Polk sent American forces under General Zachary Taylor into the disputed zone. When Mexican cavalry attacked Taylor's forces in April 1846, Polk declared that Mexico had 'shed American blood on American soil' and requested a declaration of war.

The military campaign was sweeping. American forces under Taylor won a series of battles in northern Mexico. A separate force under General Stephen Kearny seized New Mexico and California. Most dramatically, General Winfield Scott landed at Veracruz and conducted an overland campaign to Mexico City itself, capturing the Mexican capital in September 1847 after the Battle of Chapultepec — where young Mexican military cadets (the 'Niños Héroes') died defending the Military Academy, becoming iconic figures in Mexican national memory.

The Treaty of Guadalupe Hidalgo of February 1848 ended the war. Mexico ceded approximately 55 percent of its pre-war territory to the United States in exchange for $15 million and the assumption of American claims against Mexico. Mexican citizens in the ceded territories were guaranteed citizenship rights — promises that were frequently violated in practice. The war's territorial gains immediately intensified the sectional crisis over slavery, as the question of whether the new territories would permit slavery accelerated the chain of events leading to the [[event:2536|American Civil War]] a little over a decade later.
